mnemonics.title: Normal Forms Order
mnemonics.content: First Person Gives Bad Movies: 1NF (First), 2NF (Person), 3NF (Gives), BCNF (Bad), 4NF (Movies) - Helps remember normalization progression

mnemonics.title: Normalization Goal
mnemonics.content: CRUD - Create, Read, Update, Delete without anomalies. Normalization ensures all CRUD operations work correctly.

acronyms.expansion: First Normal Form - Atomic values only, no repeating groups
acronyms.usage: Check if each column has single, indivisible values

acronyms.expansion: Second Normal Form - No partial dependencies
acronyms.usage: Apply after 1NF, check for partial dependencies on composite keys

acronyms.expansion: Third Normal Form - No transitive dependencies
acronyms.usage: Apply after 2NF, ensure non-key attributes depend only on primary key

acronyms.expansion: Boyce-Codd Normal Form - Every determinant is a superkey
acronyms.usage: Stricter than 3NF, handles edge cases where 3NF fails
X → Y means X determines Y
Selected candidate key that uniquely identifies tuples

acronyms.expansion: Candidate Key - Minimal superkey that can uniquely identify tuples

visualTips.visualization: Think of a spreadsheet where each cell contains only ONE value - no lists, no arrays, no repeating columns like Phone1, Phone2, Phone3.

Use a table with composite key (A,B). Make sure attributes below depend on BOTH A and B, not just A alone. Picture a Venn diagram where non-key attributes are entirely inside the composite key circle.
Draw an arrow chain: Primary Key → Non-key Attribute 1 → Non-key Attribute 2. Break the chain! Attribute 2 should directly point to PK.
Every left-side of FD (X → Y) must be a big box (superkey) that contains all rows. If X is small (not superkey), it's a violation.

analogies.analogy: Like organizing a library: books should be categorized properly (genre), not scattered randomly. Finding, adding, or removing books becomes easier without messing up other books.
Like a fingerprint: if you know the person (X), you can identify their unique fingerprint (Y). Same person always has same fingerprint.
Like a committee where one member (part of the committee/partial key) makes decisions alone - violates the principle that full committee should decide.
Like a rumor spreading: A tells B, B tells C. C knows something because of A indirectly. In 3NF, we cut this chain - everyone reports directly to the boss (PK).
